data.title: Why are top crypto stocks RIOT, BTDR, & CIFR under pressure?
data.description:
Crypto-linked stocks just pulled back sharply, and it’s not as random as it looks. The move reflects a mix of profit booking, company-level signals, and a cooling Bitcoin (BTC-USD) trend.

  • Riot Platforms (RIOT) is down ~5% to $16.58
  • Bitdeer Technologies (BTDR) slipped ~3% to $12.23.
  • Cipher Mining (CIFR) fell ~3.28% to $17.41, marking a broad pullback across crypto-linked equities.

Riot Platform's (RIOT) decline isn’t just technical—it’s also fundamental pressure. As per its press release, the company sold ~3.78K BTC for ~$289.5M in Q1 at ~$76.6K per coin and another ~500 BTC in early April, reducing holdings to ~15.68K BTC (down ~18% YoY). This signals higher profit booking.

Technically, price got rejected near $18.5 after a sharp rally. RSI cooled to ~39, and MACD turned bearish, which means momentum cooled while selling pressure increased.

What's next: If $16 support holds, a bounce toward $20 is possible, while a break could push it to ~$14 levels.

On the other hand, Bitdeer Technologies (BTDR)  fall is more technical despite strong fundamentals. As per its press release, its March 2026 Bitcoin (BTC-USD) production jumped ~480% YoY to ~661—but price didn’t follow.

After a rally from ~$8.5 to ~$12.8, the stock showed exhaustion. The RSI eased to ~56, the MACD flipped bearish, and volumes weakened.

What's next: As long as ~11 support holds, the uptrend remains intact, with a potential retest of $15, but if it breaks, ~$9.5 might be the next stop.

At the same time, Cipher Mining’s (CIFR) drop also reflects a classic pullback. After rising from ~$13 to ~$19, the stock failed to break resistance and formed lower highs. RSI at ~45 and bearish MACD confirm short-term weakness.

What's next: Holding ~$17 support could lead to a bounce towards ~$20.5, while a breakdown may drag it toward ~$15.

Overall, the bigger picture ties back to Bitcoin (BTC-USD). According to the TradingView chart, the asset is cooling after rejection near ~$76K, now ranging ~$73K–75K. Momentum indicators show cooling, not a reversal. However, mining stocks tend to amplify BTC’s moves, so even a mild cooldown in BTC translates into sharper equity corrections. 

Other crypto-linked stocks to watch: MicroStrategy (MSTR) at ~$145.15, PayPal (PYPL) at  ~$49.61, Coinbase (COIN) at ~$199.21, Robinhood (HOOD) at ~$87.73, and CleanSpark (CLSK) at ~$11.28—currently showing more neutral price action rather than sharp downside or upside momentum.

data.title: Ambev slips after UBS warns valuation is too high
data.description:
Ambev S.A. (ABEV) fell in Thursday afternoon trading after UBS lowered its rating to Sell from Neutral on valuation concerns

Analyst Rodrigo Alcantara and his team see the risk/reward on Ambev (ABEV) as skewed to the downside following a +32% rally over the past six months and pointed to a growing disconnect between its earnings growth profile, Brazil's cost of capital, and valuation. 

"Our base case points to flat EPS growth in 2026 and a ~5% CAGR over the next five years. We see two key risks of disappointment in 2026–27. First, the macro backdrop in Brazil and global commodities may weigh on EBITDA growth/ Second, capital returns may again fall short of investor expectations, given the company's reluctance to lever its balance sheet," warned Alcantara. Due to those factors, UBS sees a risk of a de-rating in valuation multiples over the coming quarters. 

Shares of Ambev (ABEV) were down 2.6% to $3.05 at 1:20 p.m. vs. the 52-week range of $2.10 to $3.24. The Seeking Alpha Quant rating on the Brazilian brewing stock is still flashing Buy.

data.title: Adient poised to benefit from F-series production, demand for "People Carriers" -- Citi Research
data.description:
Since the conflict in the Middle East began, auto suppliers have underperformed the S&P by an average of 10%. As one of the hardest hit (down 15% relative to the S&P 500), and with fiscal Q2 results likely to beat estimates, Citi Research sees a buying opportunity in Adient (ADNT), upgrading the stock to Buy from Neutral and raising the target price by 10% to $33. 

Adient (ADNT), which supplies seating systems for the Ford (F) F-series, should benefit from a predicted 10% increase in production of the model as Ford (F) replenishes its depleted inventory after the Novelis fire.

Moreover, Citi Research analyst Michael Ward expects Adient (ADNT) to be best positioned to benefit from the increased demand of “People Carriers” (family sedans, minivans, SUVs, and CUVs) as the Millennials and Gen Zers move into the prime age group for the auto sector.

“Higher industry production and cost improvements lead to revenue and margin improvement in the second half and into 2027. In our view, improving margins and cash generation are the two most important metrics for ADNT,” Ward notes.

Ward believes the impact of the Millennial and Gen Z generations on the U.S. auto industry will be greater than the impact of the Baby Boomers in the 1980s. This suggests a “wave of demand” over the next 5-10 years as these segments (~127M people) move into the prime demographic for vehicle ownership.

Ward notes the ADNT has among the highest leverage to the “People Carrier” segment with exposure on key models for GM (GM), Toyota (TM), and Hyundai/Kia, which account for 45% of the overall “People Carrier” segment.

While near-term results will continue to reflect lower F-series production, Ward expects improvement in ADNT’s results in the second half of the year based on higher production of key North American models and the benefits of cost actions, with further gains seen in 2027.

data.title: Henry Paulson sounds alarm on potential Treasury market shock
data.description:
Former U.S. Treasury Secretary Henry Paulson is urging policymakers to prepare contingency measures to stabilize the government bond market in the event of a sudden drop in demand for U.S. Treasuries, warning such a scenario could trigger severe financial disruption.

Paulson emphasized the importance of having a rapid-response plan ready to deploy if market conditions deteriorate. Unlike the 2008 financial crisis, he noted, today’s risks are tied more directly to the scale of federal debt and the government’s ongoing financing needs. 

He cautioned that a breakdown in the roughly $31 trillion Treasury market could create a dangerous feedback loop. As investors demand higher yields to compensate for rising fiscal risks, borrowing costs would climb, further expanding budget deficits and increasing the government’s reliance on debt issuance.

In a worst-case scenario, Paulson suggested the Federal Reserve could become the primary buyer of government debt if private demand weakens significantly—an outcome that could drive bond prices lower and push interest rates higher. While the timing of such stress is uncertain, Paulson warned that the consequences could be abrupt and severe, underscoring the need for proactive planning. See how yields are trading across the curve: 

  • U.S. 2 Year Treasury yield (US2Y) trades at 3.78%.
  • U.S. 5 Year Treasury yield (US5Y) trades at 3.92%. 
  • U.S. 7 Year Treasury yield (US7Y) trades at 4.11%. 
  • U.S. 10 Year Treasury yield (US10Y) trades at 4.31%. 
  • U.S. 20 Year Treasury yield (US20Y) trades at 4.91%.
  • U.S. 30 Year Treasury yield (US30Y) trades at 4.94%. 

Fixed Income ETFs: (TLT), (TLH), (IEF), (IEI), (SHY), (SGOV), (SCHO), (BIL), (AGG), (BND), (VCIT), (MUB), (MBB), (JNK), (LQD), (HYG), (VTIP), (TIP), (SCHP), (STIP), (TIPX), (SPIP), (WIP), (GTIP), (LQDI), and (RINF).

data.title: Madison Air pops 19% in blockbuster IPO, taps AI-driven data center boom
data.description:
Madison Air Solutions Corp. (MAIR) delivered a strong debut on public markets, with its shares climbing 19% after the company raised $2.23 billion in what marks the largest U.S. industrial IPO in nearly 30 years.

The Chicago-based company saw its stock open at $32, above its offering price of $27 per share. The company sold 82.7 million shares, pricing at the top of its marketed range of $25 to $27.

Based on shares outstanding, Madison Air (MAIR) now carries a market capitalization of approximately $15.6 billion.

The listing stands as the biggest U.S. industrial offering since United Parcel Service’s $5.5 billion IPO in 1999, a deal that capitalized on investor enthusiasm during the early days of e-commerce.

Riding AI infrastructure wave

Madison Air’s (MAIR) debut comes amid growing investor appetite for companies tied to artificial intelligence infrastructure. The company supplies cooling technologies, including liquid and hybrid systems, used in data centers, a segment experiencing rapid expansion as AI workloads surge.

Demand for these systems is being fueled not only by the buildout of data centers, but also by broader trends such as the modernization of North America’s housing stock and the reshoring of advanced manufacturing. Together, these dynamics are supporting growth in what is estimated to be a $40 billion market for specialized air systems.

Industry participants note that companies connected to AI infrastructure often command higher valuations, though investors continue to assess how directly individual firms are exposed to the trend.

Broader industrial IPO revival

Madison Air (MAIR) is the latest in a series of high-profile public offerings tied to data center and infrastructure growth. Engineering firm Legence Corp. (LGN) has seen its shares surge significantly since its IPO last year, while power-equipment maker Forgent Power Solutions (FPS) has also posted gains following its debut earlier this year.

The broader enthusiasm has extended to publicly traded HVAC companies, with several stocks delivering outsized returns over the past year as investors bet on sustained demand linked to AI and energy-intensive computing.

Financials and ownership structure

Madison Air (MAIR) reported net income of $124 million on $3.34 billion in revenue for 2025, compared with $236 million in profit on $2.62 billion in revenue the prior year, reflecting both growth and margin pressure. Its top 10 customers accounted for roughly one-third of total sales.

The company was assembled by Madison Industries through a series of acquisitions starting in 2017 and employed more than 8,600 people at the end of last year.

Following the IPO, founder Larry Gies retains control through super-voting shares. Madison Industries also committed to purchase $100 million of stock in a concurrent private placement at the IPO price.

Deal details

Interest from institutional investors was strong, with several firms indicating demand for up to $525 million worth of shares prior to the offering.

The IPO was led by Goldman Sachs, Barclays, Jefferies and Wells Fargo. Madison Air’s shares trade on the New York Stock Exchange under the ticker MAIR.

data.title: Abbott forecasts 6.5%-7.5% 2026 comparable sales growth while integrating Exact Sciences with $0.20 EPS dilution
data.description:
Earnings Call Insights: Abbott Laboratories (ABT) Q1 2026

Management view

  • Robert Ford (Chairman, President & CEO) said Q1 results were “aligned with our expectations for the start of the year,” citing “adjusted earnings per share of $1.15, consistent with our guidance despite absorbing the impact of earlier than planned financing costs related to our acquisition of Exact Sciences and a weaker-than-expected respiratory season.”
  • Ford framed the Exact Sciences close as a strategic inflection point: “This acquisition adds a new high-growth business to the Abbott portfolio... expanding our presence into... Cancer Diagnostics,” and reiterated the acquisition forecast “to add approximately $3 billion of incremental sales in 2026.”
  • Ford highlighted device pipeline progress and upcoming trial activity, including “earlier-than-planned approval and launch of 2 new PFA catheters,” and preparation for multiple second-half trial starts, including “a balloon expandable TAVR valve” and “a wearable continuous lactate monitoring sensor.”
  • Philip Boudreau (CFO & Executive VP of Finance) reset Abbott’s 2026 framework to comparable growth post-acquisition: “our full year 2026 sales growth outlook of 6.5% to 7.5% is now on a comparable basis as well,” and said the updated EPS view reflects deal dilution: “our new guidance range midpoint of $5.48 reflects $0.20 of dilution related to the Exact Sciences acquisition.”

Outlook

  • Boudreau provided Q2 EPS guidance: “for the second quarter, we forecast adjusted earnings per share of $1.25 to $1.31.”
  • Ford said the company chose not to assume a late-year rebound in respiratory testing: “I didn't think it was prudent to bake that into the forecast,” while adding, “if the flu season is as aggressive as we've seen in other years, then we have the manufacturing... to be able to do that.”
  • Ford reiterated an internal expectation for a second-half acceleration: “We remain confident in our expectation for an acceleration in growth in the second half of the year,” pointing to Nutrition pricing actions, electrophysiology launches, and Core Lab momentum.

Financial results

  • Abbott reported “sales increased 3.7% on a comparable basis” and “adjusted earnings per share of $1.15 grew 6% compared to the prior year,” with Boudreau noting “foreign exchange had a favorable year-over-year impact of 4% on first quarter sales.”
  • Boudreau reported profitability and spend levels: “adjusted gross margin... 56.3% of sales,” “adjusted R&D was 6.7% of sales,” and “adjusted SG&A was 29.3% of sales.”
  • Segment and product performance highlights included Ford’s disclosure that Diabetes Care CGM sales were “$2 billion and grew 7.5%,” with growth impacted by “a delay in the renewal process related to an international tender,” and Ford’s expectation: “as we look forward to the second quarter, we expect CGM to return to double-digit growth.”

Q&A

  • David Roman, Goldman Sachs: asked how Abbott set revised guidance and risk buffers; Robert Ford (Chairman, President & CEO) said the shift to comparable reporting followed prior precedent, and on respiratory testing added, “I'm not going to forecast that we're going to make it up in Q4.”
  • Robert Marcus, JPMorgan: pressed on whether the underlying business is decelerating; Ford said he was “not sure I followed those numbers,” but emphasized portfolio diversification and said, “as a whole, the company is well set up for this year.”
  • Larry Biegelsen, Wells Fargo: challenged weak U.S. CGM prescription trends and saturation concerns; Ford replied, “I'm very bullish on the CGM market,” estimating “70 million to 80 million people... should be” on CGM vs. “around 10 million to 12 million” today.
  • Vijay Kumar, Evercore: asked how Abbott sustains Cologuard growth and whether guidance is conservative; Ford said leadership was set with “Jake Orville... reporting directly to me,” and argued demand is supported by “50 million Americans that are not up to date,” plus potential guideline shifts and international underpenetration.
  • Matthew Taylor, Jefferies: asked about Structural Heart/LAAC and competitor data; Ford said Abbott moved LAAC into EP, acknowledged “competitive intensity... in the mitral space,” and stated, “We’ve done some changes to leadership.”

Sentiment analysis

  • Analyst tone was slightly negative to skeptical on key growth engines (CGM trends, underlying organic growth, Structural Heart competition), highlighted by Biegelsen’s “concern that the current indications are saturated” and Marcus’s modeling pushback.
  • Management tone was slightly positive and confidence-forward in prepared remarks and defended assumptions in Q&A, including Ford’s “We remain confident” on second-half acceleration and his cautionary framing on respiratory season forecasting.
  • Versus Q4, management sounded more execution-focused and integration-focused (Exact Sciences now closed), while analysts shifted from forward pipeline and cadence questions toward near-term growth quality (CGM trends, organic deceleration, and segment execution).

Quarter-over-quarter comparison

  • Q4 framed 2026 using “organic sales growth” and guided full-year EPS at “$5.55 to $5.80”; Q1 shifted to comparable growth due to the acquisition, and Boudreau said the updated midpoint reflects “$0.20 of dilution.”
  • Q4 emphasized Nutrition reset actions and projected a “return to growth in the second half”; Q1 reiterated early signs with Ford saying “volume growth beginning to follow our pricing actions,” while still calling it “early in transition.”
  • Q4 highlighted pipeline approvals (Volt FDA approval and TactiFlex Duo CE Mark); Q1 discussed launches already occurring and said EP growth could “accelerate” as launches broaden.

Risks and concerns

  • Ford cited a “much weaker respiratory season” reducing Rapid and Molecular Diagnostics, and said he would not assume a Q4 testing rebound.
  • On CGM, Ford pointed to tender timing and prior-year restocking comparisons as headwinds.
  • On macro and supply chain, Ford said Middle East conflict impacts were “too early to tell” on oil costs, and Q1 effects were “more of a getting product into the region kind of impact,” with mitigation via inventory positioning.

Final takeaway

Management described Q1 as on-plan despite weaker respiratory testing and earlier financing costs tied to the now-closed Exact Sciences acquisition, and maintained a second-half acceleration narrative anchored in Nutrition volume recovery actions, broader electrophysiology PFA launches, and Core Lab momentum. The updated 2026 framing moved to comparable sales growth of 6.5% to 7.5% with $0.20 of EPS dilution from Exact Sciences, while Q&A centered on investor scrutiny of CGM trajectory, underlying growth quality, and segment execution in Structural Heart and Diagnostics China.

Read the full Earnings Call Transcript

data.title: U.S. stocks advance after making new highs as investors await U.S.-Iran peace deal
data.description:
Wall Street's major averages rose on Thursday, after the S&P 500 ((SP500)) and the Nasdaq ((COMP:IND)) had reached new records on hopes that the U.S.-Iran war could be over soon.

The benchmark S&P 500 (SP500) was +0.1%, while the heavy-tech Nasdaq Composite (COMP:IND) was +0.2%, and the blue-chip Dow (DJI) was flat.

Over in the bond market, the benchmark 10-year Treasury yield (US10Y) was 2 basis points higher at 4.31%, while the 2-year Treasury yield (US2Y) rose 1 basis point to 3.78%.

Crude oil futures (CL1:COM) were slightly higher at $94, while Brent (CO1:COM) was at $99 per barrel.

Multiple media reports said President Donald Trump indicated the “war is close to over,” fueling optimism that easing geopolitical tensions could support risk appetite heading into the session. Consequently, Trump announced that Israel and Lebanon agreed on a 10-day ceasefire.

The U.S. and Iran are expected to return to Pakistan next week for a second round of peace talks, according to two senior Pakistani officials involved in finalizing decisions with both the U.S. and Iranian teams, reports said.

On the economic front, the Philadelphia Fed Manufacturing Index unexpectedly rose in April. The initial jobless claims dipped slightly more than expected in the past week.

Also, industrial production decreased by 0.5% MoM in March, against the +0.1% consensus, according to the Federal Reserve.

data.title: Global oil supply will not snap back quickly after Iran war, Phillips 66 CEO Lashier says
data.description:
The "tail effect" of oil disruptions in the Middle East will haunt the markets for months, if not years—with some effects bouncing back quickly, while others will take longer—Phillips 66 (PSX) CEO Mark Lashier told Yahoo Finance at the Semafor World Economy Summit on Wednesday.

A Middle East ceasefire would not be enough to fix a global energy system where damage has already been done, Lashier said. "You'll have to redesign things, build things to repair."

When the flow stops from a waterway that transits ~20% of the world's crude oil and liquid natural gas, the world cannot simply pivot, since "the world has become very integrated and very efficient," the CEO said.

While Saudi Arabia and the United Arab Emirates can move oil through their respective pipelines, ~12M bbl/day are effectively trapped, and "I don't think we've seen the full impact of that disruption yet," Lashier warned.

The result is a massive logistical shift, the CEO said, as Asian buyers have been forced to aggressively acquire North American and Atlantic Basin crudes to fill the void—a global reshuffling that is expensive, inefficient, and perhaps permanent.

Phillips 66 (PSX) shares have slipped 8% over the past 30 days but have surged nearly 70% in the past year.

data.title: Devon Energy on track to enter green territory after six straight sessions of losses
data.description:
Devon Energy (DVN) was on track to snap six straight sessions of losses on Thursday as its shares rose 1.77% to trade at $46.03.

The energy major started its fall on April 8 when it closed 4.08% lower at $47.91. It lost nearly 6% between April 8 and April 15. On a YTD basis, however, shares of the oil company have risen over 25% in stark comparison to a nearly 3% rise in the wider S&P 500 index.

Looking at Seeking Alpha’s quant rating, the stock has been rated a Hold with a score of 3.42 out of 5. DVN has been rated an A for its profitability but a D for its growth. Seeking Alpha analysts, on the other hand, were bullish on the stock and recommended to Buy it. Wall Street analysts mirrored a similar sentiment with a Buy call, with 21 analysts rating the stock a Buy or above.

Seeking Alpha analyst The Asian Investor rated the stock as Strong Buy, expecting it to benefit from elevated oil prices driven by escalating Middle East conflict and U.S.-Iran tensions and its recent merger with Coterra Energy.

The merger will enable the energy company to expand its production footprint, deepen its presence in natural gas, and unlock meaningful merger synergies, the analyst noted.

However, High Alpha Professor rated it Hold, arguing that the stock is fairly valued with an upside bias.

“The rally we have seen may not hold, but the underlying business is now stronger. I, therefore, consider DVN a Hold at current levels, with the possibility of upside if oil prices stay near $100, while downside is supported by structural improvements we can expect from the merger, including the capital returns,” it added.

data.title: Eton Pharma drops after CFO succession plan
data.description:
• Shares of Eton Pharmaceuticals (ETON) fell on Thursday after the company announced that its current Chief Financial Officer, James Gruber, is leaving next month as part of a succession plan.
  • Judith “Judy” M. Matthews, a former CFO of Irish drugmaker Iterum Therapeutics (ITRMF), will become executive vice president of accounting and finance before assuming the CFO role effective June 1, the company said.
  • “On behalf of the entire Company, I want to thank James for his contributions and dedication over the last four years,” Eton (ETON) CEO Sean Brynjelsen remarked.
  • Gruber, who joined ETON as CFO in April 2022, will remain in his current role until May 31 before entering into a six-month consulting agreement with the company.
  • “He provided exceptional leadership to our finance department through a period of rapid growth and numerous product integrations,” Brynjelsen added.
